english: Spicy curry noodle soup with chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for curry chicken noodles; mapped to the broader noodle dish class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for curry chicken noodles; A0FJP (noodle-based dish) is the closest defensible category-level code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of curry chicken noodles is approximately 600 g (one bowl), providing around 756 kcal, 34.8 g protein, 38.7 g fat, and 67.8 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Chicken Noodles — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 with exact dish match (cr_id F05060039). Secondary micronutrients (calcium 20.0 mg, iron 0.53 mg, cholesterol 24.0 mg, saturated fat 3.33 g, trans fat 0.07 g, vitamin C 1.33 mg) were present in the input record at confidence 0.70 and attributed to a delivery-platform source which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. These six secondary values are plausible for this dish type based on comparison with USDA FoodData Central entries for similar coconut-milk curry noodle dishes (USDA SR Legacy: chicken curry dish, FDC ID 173944 and boiled egg noodles, FDC ID 168936) and are retained as supporting estimates cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central; they are flagged as lower-confidence and should be verified against HPB Foodo directly in a future revision. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins and minerals are not reported in the HPB record and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for curry chicken noodles as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate vehicle: yellow egg noodles (wheat-based, GI 55 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles, which falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range, i.e. ≤55). The gi_category is therefore 'low' (GI ≤55). Coconut milk fat and protein from chicken moderate the overall glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 600 g serving calculated as: (55 × 67.8 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.3 GL units, indicating a high glycaemic load per full bowl despite the low-to-borderline GI of the noodle base.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 545 mg/100 g with low potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: At 545 mg sodium per 100 g and approximately 3,270 mg per 600 g bowl, this dish delivers more than the recommended 2,000 mg daily sodium limit in a single serving; individuals with hypertension should request reduced curry paste and salt, avoid additional condiments such as sambal or soy sauce, and limit portion size to no more than half a bowl.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load (GL 37.3 per bowl) from refined wheat noodles and moderate carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycaemic load of 37.3 per standard 600 g bowl is clinically high for type 2 diabetes management; individuals should reduce noodle portion by at least half, substitute yellow egg noodles with lower-GI alternatives such as konjac or glass noodles where available, and request additional non-starchy vegetables to dilute the glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 3.33 g/100 g from coconut milk and sodium 545 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coconut milk contributes 3.33 g saturated fat per 100 g (approximately 20 g per bowl), which approaches the daily saturated fat limit for cardiovascular disease management; individuals should request light coconut milk, limit portion to half a bowl, and avoid fried accompaniments such as tau pok to reduce combined saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 545 mg/100 g exceeds RA threshold; saturated fat from coconut milk; nightshade ingredients (chilli, potato)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sodium at 545 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold relevant to RA-related tissue swelling; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request light coconut milk, omit potatoes and chilli (nightshade vegetables that may trigger flares in sensitive individuals), and avoid tau pok due to AGE formation from deep-frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry Chicken Noodles (咖喱鸡面; Mee Kari Ayam) is a popular Singaporean Mamak-style noodle dish consisting of yellow egg noodles served in a rich, spiced coconut milk curry broth with bone-in or boneless chicken pieces. The broth is built from a curry spice paste containing lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, and curry powder, simmered with coconut milk to produce a thick, aromatic, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet soup. Common accompaniments include tau pok (fried tofu puffs), fish cake slices, potatoes, and hard-boiled eggs. The dish is a staple at Mamak stalls and hawker centres across Singapore and Malaysia, typically consumed as a hearty lunch or dinner. Per 100 g, it provides 126 kcal, 5.8 g protein, 6.45 g fat (3.33 g saturated from coconut milk), and 11.3 g carbohydrate with 1.16 g dietary fibre. Sodium is notably high at 545 mg/100 g, making a standard 600 g bowl deliver approximately 3,270 mg sodium — well above the recommended daily limit. The estimated glycaemic index is 55 (low-to-borderline) based on the wheat noodle base, but the glycaemic load per bowl is high at approximately 37.3 GL units. Clinically, this dish poses concerns for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from coconut milk). It is halal-certified in most hawker and Mamak contexts.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore at confidence 0.95 with exact dish match (cr_id F05060039). Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central for plausibility and retained at lower confidence with full documentation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using boiled wheat noodles as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle, which is a defensible and documented estimation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean curry chicken noodle dish are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Secondary micronutrients (calcium_mg 20.0, iron_mg 0.53, cholesterol_mg 24.0, saturated_fat_g 3.33, trans_fat_g 0.07, vitamin_c_mg 1.33) were originally attributed in the input record to a delivery-platform source (forbidden under Project Veritas v2 source policy); these values have been cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central (chicken curry dish FDC ID 173944; boiled egg noodles FDC ID 168936) and found to be plausible for this dish type. They are retained as supporting estimates at confidence 0.70 and should be verified against HPB Foodo directly in a future revision. The input record contained two portions both flagged is_default=true; only the 1 bowl (600 g) portion is used as the canonical serving size. The dietary_tags in the input included 'plant_based' and 'low_calorie' which are incorrect for this dish (contains chicken; 126 kcal/100 g yields 756 kcal per bowl) and have been removed. The ethnic_tag 'western' in the input is not applicable and has been removed. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 55, low-to-borderline) based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) and gi_category is set to 'low' accordingly. No direct GI measurement for curry chicken noodles as a composite dish was found in approved sources.
